abstract:
This paper examines the methodological frameworks and assumptions used by European Union member states to evaluate the economic and social costs of road accidents. While EU directives advocate for the valuation of road incidents as a cornerstone of traffic safety management, the practical application of these standards varies significantly. Disparities in data accessibility across member states influence the granularity of valuation and the specific categorization of unit costs. Consequently, road accident cost estimates remain among the most difficult parameters to harmonize, complicating cross-border comparisons. 

Crucially, the most significant dimension of accident evaluation is the humanistic one—the quantification of non-material impacts such as pain, suffering, and social exclusion. This study explores the fundamental challenges associated with calculating key cost categories, prioritized by data availability, and analyzes several critical valuation metrics within a broader social context, specifically focusing on the complex determination of the value of statistical life.
